beliefs.stance: Agentic technologies promised cost savings haven't delivered; offshoring has accelerated instead of slowing down, exemplifying a Cobra Effect where perverse incentives lead to opposite outcomes.
beliefs.evidence_quote: Ironically, that gap has accelerated offshoring rather than slowing it down. In many cases, companies made cost-cutting commitments to boards and leadership based on these technologies, and when the ROI didn’t materialize, offshoring became the fallback strategy.
beliefs.opposes: The mainstream consensus that AI will significantly reduce contact center costs.

beliefs.stance: Autonomy is earned, not granted; most companies skip this part and pay for it later. High talent density allows teams to find breakthroughs without prescribed steps, while low talent density can quietly blow up a company.
beliefs.evidence_quote: With high talent density, you can give the team a big problem, a small problem, or even something repetitive. They'll find breakthroughs without you having to prescribe steps. With low talent density, the same freedom can quietly blow up your company.
beliefs.opposes: The popular belief that hiring great people and then getting out of their way is sufficient for success.

beliefs.stance: Market-Product Fit (MPF) is crucial, not Product-Market Fit (PMF); aligning product with market needs before ensuring the market exists for the product.
beliefs.evidence_quote: Market-Product Fit, not Product-Market Fit.
beliefs.opposes: The traditional belief that prioritizing product alignment with a pre-existing market is more important.

beliefs.stance: Broken systems trace to broken incentives. Narrative fit matters more than metrics.
beliefs.evidence_quote: When everyone builds the same thing, returns distribute thin. Image risk > capital risk for VCs.

beliefs.evidence_quote: Historically: Services = linear scaling, “uncool,” anti-venture. Product = leverage, scalability, “real tech.” But AI has flipped the script.

beliefs.stance: Emotional intelligence is super important to build & scale the mission of empowering a billion voices with speech AI.
beliefs.evidence_quote: Here’s how I thought about our rounds at Sanas: Seed: Prove the product works. Series A: Prove the market wants it, and sign every major BPO. Series B: Own speech understanding as a category. Hiring a head of finance too early is like bringing on a translator before you know what language you’re speaking.

beliefs.stance: Each layer reinforces the next. Each touchpoint compounds our reach.
beliefs.evidence_quote: Here’s how it often plays out: We get adopted at a contact center. They introduce us to their customer, the brand. The brand sees success and brings us into other centers they work with, across regions, operations, and use cases.

beliefs.stance: AI isn’t replacing human agents, it’s making their role more concentrated & filtered. The human agents are no longer the first line of support.
beliefs.evidence_quote: We now have agents (quite literally) everywhere. Assistance is low-cost, fast, and always-on. That’s great, but it's also changing human behavior in subtle but important ways. Because of low-cost and always-on agents, brands are increasing touch-points with their customers - chat, voice bots, interactive FAQs, self-serve flows.
beliefs.opposes: Human agents as first line of support

beliefs.stance: Perpetual dissatisfaction ≠ visionary leadership. When everyone builds the same thing, returns distribute thin.
beliefs.evidence_quote: There’s a crazy rush right now in AI adoption across the enterprise. Getting in the door is easier than it’s ever been. The hard part is staying in! Your net retention is your core revenue driver.
beliefs.opposes: Visionary leadership as key to success

beliefs.stance: If you're attending mega-events like CCW just to generate leads, you've already missed the point.

beliefs.opposes: Event attendance solely for lead generation

beliefs.stance: These aren’t marketing lines. These are real voices which are finally being heard, without being judged for how they sound.

beliefs.stance: For our users, Sanas isn’t just a tool. It’s a vehicle for dignity, opportunity, and economic mobility.

stories.title: Entry-level roles as R&D labs for leadership development
stories.summary: At 19 years old, the founder was moonlighting at a call center while studying in Bangalore. This entry-level job not only provided income but also discovered the problem that would be solved over the next 20 years and built two unicorns around it.
stories.contrast_pair: AI replacing entry-level roles vs preserving leadership development pipeline
key_quotes: That 19-year-old kid is the reason a million agents have Sanas running on their desktops today.

stories.title: The importance of trust surface in startup growth
stories.summary: Every startup's growth is bounded by how many people the founder can trust with important outcomes. A founder who trusts 8 people in a company of 500 has built an 8-person company, with 492 people waiting for direction.
stories.contrast_pair: Trust surface under 10 vs organization building
key_quotes: If your trust surface is under 10 people in a company over 200, you're not building an organization. You're building a waiting room.

stories.title: AI commoditizing the software moat of companies
stories.summary: Technology is roughly 5% of most companies' moat. AI replicates this 5%, but doesn't touch the other 95% - proprietary data, distribution relationships, regulatory positioning, operational integration.
stories.contrast_pair: Software moat vs structural conditions that made a company defensible
key_quotes: AI replicates the 5%. It doesn't touch the 95%.

stories.title: Series B Rejection Narrative
stories.summary: Despite strong ARR and zero churn over six months with Fortune 100 customers, the business was still rejected by investors due to narrative misalignment. The story highlights how investors evaluated based on mainstream narratives rather than objective metrics.
stories.contrast_pair: Objective metrics vs investor narrative evaluation
stories.key_quotes: "The numbers were real - strong ARR, zero churn over six months, Fortune 100 customers, gross margins that made enterprise software investors sit up."

stories.title: 106 Flights/Year Story
stories.summary: The founder's obsessive approach of face-to-face meetings with customers, totaling 106 flights per year, demonstrates the importance of personal engagement in building strong relationships and understanding customer needs.
stories.key_quotes: "106 flights/year."

stories.title: Call Center at 19, Bangalore Origin Story
stories.summary: Starting a call center at the age of 19 in Bangalore marks an early entry into the industry and highlights how personal experiences can shape career paths.
stories.key_quotes: "Assigned the name Nathan at 19 at a call center because his accent wasn't good enough."

stories.title: Series B Rejection Contrarian Story
stories.summary: The founder's experience of being rejected by investors despite strong metrics and customer base illustrates the importance of aligning narratives with investor expectations, highlighting a contrarian approach.
stories.contrast_pair: Objective business performance vs misaligned narrative evaluation
stories.key_quotes: "They weren't wrong about the numbers. They were wrong about the narrative."

stories.title: Retention Optimization Misalignment Story
stories.summary: The founder's observation that retention optimization often narrows who a product works for, rather than broadening its appeal, underscores the need to focus on acquisition alongside retention.
stories.key_quotes: "Most retention optimization is actually just narrowing who your product works for."

stories.title: Interview Process Talent vs Comfort Story
stories.summary: The founder's insight into the interview process reveals how it rewards people adept at joining organizations rather than talent, emphasizing the importance of recognizing true capability over performance in comfort.
stories.key_quotes: "The interview process rewards people who've learned how to be interviewed. What you're selecting for, with ruthless efficiency, is not talent - it's people who are excellent at joining organizations."

stories.title: Series B Contrarian Narrative Story
stories.summary: The founder's recount of the Series B rejection due to narrative misalignment highlights how investors evaluate based on mainstream narratives rather than objective metrics, emphasizing the importance of aligning one's story with investor expectations.
stories.contrast_pair: Objective business performance vs misaligned narrative evaluation
stories.key_quotes: "The investors who passed weren't evaluating our business. They were evaluating whether our narrative fit the story they'd need to tell their LPs if it didn't work out."

stories.title: Solo Developer Billion-Dollar Myth Story
stories.summary: The founder debunks the myth of solo developers creating billion-dollar companies, emphasizing that true success often involves years of unsexy grinding and compounding expertise hitting the market at the right moment.
stories.contrast_pair: Solo hacking vs decade of unglamorous work
stories.key_quotes: "The media loves weekend hacks. They rarely cover the decade of unglamorous work that makes 'overnight success' inevitable."

stories.title: Startup Media Survivorship Bias Story
stories.summary: The founder critiques startup media for creating survivorship bias by covering outliers rather than averages, leading new founders to optimize for exceptions instead of rules.
stories.key_quotes: "Every story about a founder who "prioritized mental health" and "maintained work-life balance" while building a unicorn creates a generation of founders who think that's the path."

stories.title: Sanas' $50M ARR Milestone
stories.summary: Sanas grew to a $50M ARR company in 2025, marking its growth and profitability. The founder expresses pride and excitement for the achievement.
key_quotes: It was exhausting, invigorating, exciting, and incredibly fulfilling in the end. Yet the journey still feels like it’s just at the starting line.

stories.title: Sanas' Daytona Moment
stories.summary: Sanas faced initial backlash as a 'racist software', but overcame to become omnipresent in the CX world, marking its 'Daytona' moment. The founder marks this occasion.
key_quotes: From $0 to $50M+ ARR in 10 quarters... And in the last 6 months, we have seen zero churn, with half of our customers paying us over $1M.

stories.title: Founder Salary Advice
stories.summary: The founder advises founders to pay themselves properly, emphasizing that desperation doesn't breed better decision-making. He contrasts those who accept $0 salary with those who make a living wage.
key_quotes: Desperation doesn't create good judgment. It breeds desperation. And desperate founders burn capital faster than confident ones.

stories.title: Comfort Feels Kind But It's Cruel
stories.summary: The founders I know who broke through $50M and $100M cultures ran honest ones, where feedback was specific. Comfort feels kind but it's actually the cruelest thing you can offer someone capable of more.
key_quotes: Comfort feels kind, but it's actually the cruelest thing you can offer someone who's capable of more.

stories.title: Lens Through Early Hires
stories.summary: Your early team doesn't just execute your vision. They filter what information reaches you, shape which opportunities you even see. By the time you realize it, you're not leading the company anymore; you're being led by the logic your early decisions installed.
stories.contrast_pair: Early hires shaping lens vs leadership
key_quotes: Hire people who optimize for investor narratives, and you stop seeing customer problems. You start seeing fundable narratives.

stories.title: Intensity Compounds Exponentially
stories.summary: No company suffers from too much founder intensity reaching the front lines; they suffer when middle management protects their team from that intensity. Intensity compounds exponentially, and dropping a few percentiles makes you irrelevant.
key_quotes: Intensity compounds exponentially. When you move from 95th percentile effort to 99th, you don't get 4% better results. You get 10x or 100x better results.

stories.title: Products Need Soul vs Enterprise Procurement
stories.summary: The 'products need soul' crowd is about to learn a painful lesson about enterprise procurement. You know what never came up in a single procurement review? Whether our product 'felt alive.' Uptime, security, integration complexity, total cost of ownership always came up.
key_quotes: You know what never came up in a single procurement review? Whether our product 'felt alive.' You know what always came up? Uptime, security, integration complexity, total cost of ownership.

stories.title: CEOs Are Humans Too
stories.summary: When you walk into a meeting treating executives like their title, you're reinforcing that armor. When you treat them like a human, you're being refreshing. Stop selling to the position; start talking to the person who remembers what it was like before the title.
key_quotes: When you walk into a meeting treating them like their title, you're reinforcing that armor. When you treat them like a human, you're being refreshing.

stories.title: Learning Velocity Matters
stories.summary: Nobody talks about why learning velocity actually matters. It's not because the field moves fast; it's because the field moves in unpredictable directions. If AI was just moving fast in a straight line, experienced people would catch up.
stories.contrast_pair: Straight-line progress vs unpredictable direction learning
key_quotes: That's the actual skill: not learning but unlearning. Discarding knowledge you worked hard to acquire because it's now actively hurting you.

stories.title: Anant's Leadership Journey
stories.summary: Anant S., from Baroda to San Francisco, rebuilt his career in the US and became a sales leader at Sanas, helping scale revenue from $2M ARR to $50M ARR.
key_quotes: I've always found Anant's story very inspirational, it's a Baroda to San Francisco story of grit & resilience.
Back then, we were at $2M ARR. Today, we’re at $50M ARR.

stories.title: Sales Leader Selection
stories.summary: Shawn chose Anant as Chief Sales Officer for Sanas, recognizing his ability to build GTM motion and scale revenue from $2M ARR to $50M ARR.
stories.contrast_pair: Founder's vision vs. experienced sales leader
key_quotes: He said, 'You bet.' Back then, we were at $2M ARR. Today, we’re at $50M ARR.

stories.title: Sales Leader's Impact
stories.summary: Anant celebrates team wins louder than his own and knows true success is measured in customer friendships, champions, and advocates.
key_quotes: He celebrates the team’s wins louder than his own, and he knows that true success is measured in customers who become friends, champions, and advocates.

stories.title: Sales Leader's Learning Mindset
stories.summary: Anant embodies the best people: comfortable with uncertainty, asking more questions than answering, defaulting to 'let me figure that out' instead of pretending expertise.
key_quotes: The best people I've worked with have the opposite energy. They're comfortable with uncertainty. They ask more questions than they answer. They default to 'let me figure that out' instead of pretending they already know.

stories.title: Sales Leader's Hiring Philosophy
stories.summary: Shawn reflects on the dangerous hires who had all the answers, contrasting them with the best people who are comfortable with uncertainty and ask more questions.
key_quotes: The most dangerous hires I've made were the ones who had all the answers.
Here's what separates them: they've hit enough problems to know how much they don't know.

stories.title: Operator vs. Founder Dynamics
stories.summary: Founders bring vision and courage while operators provide structure, momentum, and repeatability to scale the founder's spark.

stories.title: Operator's Role in Scaling
stories.summary: Operators exist to make founders wildly successful by turning ambition into operating cadence and translating ideas into accountable rhythms.

stories.title: Founder's Vision and Operator's Structure
stories.summary: When founder vision connects with operator structure, clarity sharpens, execution accelerates, and growth becomes inevitable rather than accidental.
stories.contrast_pair: Founders' Vision vs. Operators' Structure & Momentum

stories.title: Operator's Impact on Founder Success
stories.summary: Operators exist to elevate founders' vision without diluting its soul, building systems that let founders stay in their genius zone while the business scales responsibly.
stories.contrast_pair: Founder's Genius Zone vs. Business Scaling

stories.title: Founder's Growth Milestone
stories.summary: The company grew from $0 to $40M in ARR under Shawn and Anant's leadership.
stories.contrast_pair: Starting with no revenue vs achieving significant growth
key_quotes: I hired my leadership team not just for competence but for their ability to build relationships. Anant can walk into any room and read it. That's political savvy, and it's why we've grown from $0 to $40M in ARR.

stories.title: Founder's Humility and Influence
stories.summary: Sowmyanarayan Sampath, Verizon Consumer's CEO, emphasized purpose over metrics during a meeting with Shawn.
stories.contrast_pair: Authority vs genuine curiosity in leadership
key_quotes: From the moment the conversation began, he shifted the focus away from himself, away from titles and metrics, and directly toward purpose. He asked two simple and foundational questions: Why do you really want to partner with Verizon? Why are you building Sanas? The entire 90 minute conversation felt grounded in service, not authority.

stories.title: Founder's Market Timing Decision
stories.summary: The founders started Sanas when everyone said that contact centers would die.
stories.contrast_pair: Going against popular narratives vs following trends
key_quotes: We went against the narratives flooded by thought leaders and we built a real business.

stories.title: Founder's Humility vs Softness
stories.summary: Sampath reminded that humility is not softness but clarity, strength, and the ability to make others the center of the room.
key_quotes: Leaders like Sampath remind you that humility is not softness. It is clarity, it is strength and it is the ability to make someone else the center of the room so the best ideas can rise & deeper relationships can be built.

stories.title: Founder's Metrics vs Invisible Work
stories.summary: Some of Shawn's best performers weren't those crushing visible metrics but those who couldn't be quantified.
stories.contrast_pair: Quantifiable performance vs unmeasured high value work
key_quotes: Some of my best performers weren't the ones crushing visible metrics, they were the ones I couldn't quantify. For example, spending disproportionately high time with a customer to convert him in our biggest champion.

stories.title: Founder's Trend Analysis
stories.summary: Founders talk about building AI for X industry every week, but the trend becomes crowded and returns get distributed.
stories.contrast_pair: Early vs late entry in a crowded trend
key_quotes: Everyone's building the same company right now. Five years ago, they all had different ideas. Now? Everyone's pitching some version of 'AI for X industry.' Different words, same thesis.

stories.title: Founder's Invisible Work Recognition
stories.summary: Metrics break companies because unmeasured high value work becomes invisible and gets no recognition.
key_quotes: There's always a person who spots a disaster coming and quietly fixes it. Unfortunately, in only metrics driven organisation, this person gets no recognition. There's no KPI for 'averted crisis.' Meanwhile, someone who hits their number, often by creating future problems, gets promoted.

stories.title: Founder's Customer Conversion Story
stories.summary: Spending disproportionately high time with a customer to convert him into the company's biggest champion.
stories.contrast_pair: High time investment vs visible metrics
key_quotes: Sure, we should quatify inputs and outputs, but often introduction of metrics makes unmeasured work as in work becomes invisible work. And invisible work feels like no work.

stories.title: Founder's Market Waiting Decision
stories.summary: Waiting for perfect market conditions often leads to missed opportunities.
key_quotes: Waiting for perfect market conditions is how you miss every opportunity. There's never a perfect time to build. Markets are always either too hot or too cold. Timing the market is often how you lose.

stories.title: Founder's Company Building Decision
stories.summary: Building something that people need, not being obsessed about narratives, creates value.
stories.contrast_pair: Obsession with narratives vs building for customers
key_quotes: Great companies get built in terrible markets. Because they build something that people need. Stop waiting for the market to be ready. Start building.

stories.title: Protecting Innovators' Rights
stories.summary: Sanas filed a lawsuit to protect their rights after realizing the need for decisive action when leading an innovator-focused company. The story highlights the importance of defending intellectual property and innovation, especially in industries where copycats are inevitable.
stories.contrast_pair: Standing strong vs potential loss of innovative tools
key_quotes: We have built tools that bring people together and improve lives. If we don’t defend our rights, we put that kind of innovation at risk, and that’s bad for everyone.
When copying crosses the line into stealing, there have to be consequences.

stories.title: Sanas' first consumer product launch
stories.summary: Sanas launched real-time language translation as its first consumer product. The founder feels most confident speaking in his mother tongue, Sankethi (Sanskrit), and aims to see the first conversation between his son and parents in Sankethi.
key_quotes: I feel most confident speaking in my mother tongue, Sankethi (Sanskrit). It’s what we speak at home. My son understands it, but doesn’t speak it, a familiar story for many immigrant families where the language gap grows across generations.

That’s my ‘why.’ But the need for a language bridge goes far beyond me: Brilliant developers held back by limited English. Patients waiting 30 minutes for a live interpreter just to have a 5-minute conversation with a doctor. 911 callers waiting for over 7 minutes in case they need non-English responder on the calls. 7 minutes in an emergency is an eternity. Businesses locked out of markets, for example only ~5% of Brazil speaks English.

stories.title: Sanas' response to opponent's shocking claims
stories.summary: Sanas publicly disclosed details of lawsuits against an opponent in the United States District Court and High Court of Delhi, India. The opponent responded with shocking claims, such as suggesting it should be illegal for Sanas to give away their noise cancellation software for free.
key_quotes: Our opponent now says it should be illegal for Sanas to give away our noise cancellation software for free. It isn’t, and we aren’t about to back down.

stories.title: Real-time translation as common as captions on video calls
stories.summary: The founder predicts that in two years, real-time language translation will be as common as captions on video calls. Sanas aims to make conversations personal again without delay or a third-party interpreter.
key_quotes: In two years, real-time language translation will be as common as captions on video calls.

From the beginning, Sanas has been about removing the friction that blocks people from understanding each other. And this launch brings us one step closer to a world where no voice is lost in translation.

stories.title: Sanas' impact on diverse use cases
stories.summary: Real-time translation allows people to speak in their own voice and emotion without delay or a third-party interpreter, making conversations personal again. It benefits brilliant developers held back by limited English, patients waiting for live interpreters, 911 callers needing non-English responders, and businesses locked out of markets.
key_quotes: Real-time translation lets people speak in their own voice and emotion, without delay or a third-party interpreter. It makes conversations personal again.

Brilliant developers held back by limited English. Patients waiting 30 minutes for a live interpreter just to have a 5-minute conversation with a doctor. 911 callers waiting for over 7 minutes in case they need non-English responder on the calls. 7 minutes in an emergency is an eternity. Businesses locked out of markets, for example only ~5% of Brazil speaks English.

stories.title: Growth Milestone from $0 to $30M ARR
stories.summary: The founder recounts the growth milestone from founding to scaling, emphasizing the challenges and strategies that led to reaching $30 million in annual revenue.
stories.key_quotes: "The growth milestone from founding to scale"

stories.title: 106 Flights/Year Obsessive Customer Proximity
stories.summary: The founder emphasizes the importance of face-to-face meetings with customers, noting that they flew over 100 times a year to maintain close proximity and understand customer needs.
stories.key_quotes: "106 flights/year — Obsessive customer proximity, face-to-face meetings"

stories.title: Call Center Origin Story
stories.summary: The founder shares the origin story of starting a call center at age 19 in Bangalore, detailing how this experience shaped their entry into the industry.
stories.key_quotes: "Call center at 19, Bangalore — Origin story, entry into the industry"

stories.title: Autonomy Earned, Not Granted
stories.summary: The founder discusses the importance of autonomy being earned rather than granted in companies, highlighting the pitfalls of skipping this foundational step.
stories.key_quotes: "Autonomy is earned, not granted. Most companies skip that part, and pay for it later"

stories.title: Sanas Talent Density Strategy
stories.summary: The founder outlines Sanas' approach to talent density, over-communication, context sharing, and control of blast radius to foster autonomy.
stories.contrast_pair: Talent density vs low talent density
stories.key_quotes:
"1. Talent density - Raise the bar, so default judgment is high
2. No context gap - Over-communicate, share the “why,” the risks, the constraints, and the scars from past failures.
3. Control the blast radius - Innovate in bounded zones so mistakes don’t sink the whole company"

stories.title: AI Customer Service Reality Check
stories.summary: The founder debunks the narrative that AI will take over customer service, emphasizing its limitations and the actual impact on contact centers.
stories.contrast_pair: AI takeover vs reality of contact center work
stories.key_quotes:
"I’m not speaking as an outsider. I’ve been a CX agent, built products in this space, invested in both automation and augmentation tech, and founded two unicorns. So, I’m not guessing, I’m reporting from the field.
Sure, agentic startups are building incredible products and are gaining momentum. But they’re mostly handling first-touch, rules-based, low-EQ tasks. That work never belonged with human agents to begin with.
In fact, there’s been no meaningful automation of conversations over 2 minutes that require both IQ and EQ. Much of what we’re seeing is “voice as the new keyboard,” not true call deflection"

stories.title: Founder Funded vs Not Funded Story
stories.summary: The founder contrasts experiences of founders who get funded versus those who don't, focusing on the importance of lived experiences over abstract storytelling.
stories.key_quotes:
"Here's the difference between a founder who gets funded and one who doesn't:
We have all learned that we need to tell stories, but most of those stories are too abstract, they are not lived experiences.
"We implemented in a fortune 500 company, and went live in 2 months, the ROI was XX"
This is not a lived experience, that's a rhetoric. The lived experience is something line this:
"We signed up our biggest customer not by an intro, but by a cold call to their India leadership. They shrugged it off earlier, but we kept sending case studies, industry reports, and then one day they heard about us from their customer. And then they called us, negotiated a very small pilot, and signed up. We thought we had won a massive account, but it took similar effort for their other regions, and eventually all those local wins led us to an invite from global leadership, which now had multiple ROI case studies from their own company, so a global rollout was the most obvious step.""

stories.title: Mediocrity Kills Startups
stories.summary: The founder discusses how talent density is crucial for startup success and how cultures that lack it fail.
stories.key_quotes:
"Every culture deck loves “candor, learning, freedom, responsibility.” But none of it works without talent density.
